frameworks.name: dynamic Bayesian inference
frameworks.one_line: agent infers hidden environmental state by combining prediction with new sensory evidence

frameworks.name: internal state transition model
frameworks.one_line: action-dependent forward model used to predict hidden states across time

frameworks.name: probabilistic population decoding
frameworks.one_line: decoding method used to read goal-distance representations from cortical populations

core_question: Does posterior parietal cortex implement the prediction-and-update steps of dynamic Bayesian inference during a goal-reaching task?
key_assumptions: dynamic Bayesian inference decomposes into prediction via a transition model and update with sensory evidence
PPC and adjacent PM jointly support inference of hidden environmental state
anticipatory licking reflects internal estimates of goal distance
manipulations: intermittent omission of acoustic cue sounds during virtual navigation
optogenetic-style silencing of PPC during the goal-reaching task
imaging across PPC and PM layers 2, 3 and 5
key_findings: anticipatory licking increased near reward even when sound cues were intermittently omitted
PPC silencing disrupted the cue-omission anticipatory licking
PPC and PM neurons represented goal distance during sound omission, especially in PPC layers 3 and 5
goal-distance prediction improved when cue sounds were observed
interpretations: PPC and PM implement the two fundamental operations of dynamic Bayesian inference: prediction and updating
cerebral cortex realizes mental simulation through an action-dependent dynamic model
